Understanding Business Activity.definition: An organization that produces goods or services to satisfy customer needs and wants while making a profit.
A person who takes the financial risk of starting and managing a new venture.
Money invested in a business by the owners.
Another word for business, often used when describing a new business.
A person who purchases goods and services for personal use.
Basic requirements essential for survival, such as food, water, shelter, and clothing.
Desires for goods and services that are not necessary for survival but improve quality of life.
The challenge of allocating limited resources to satisfy unlimited wants.
The resources used to produce goods and services: land, labour, capital, and enterprise.
Natural resources used in production, such as minerals, forests, water, and land area.
Human effort, physical and mental, used in the production of goods and services.
A situation where resources are insufficient to meet all human wants.
The next best alternative forgone when making a decision.
Focusing production on a limited range of goods or services to gain efficiency.
Splitting production into separate tasks and assigning each worker a specific task.
The difference between the selling price of a product and the cost of bought-in materials and components.
Organizations engaged in producing goods or services to satisfy needs and wants.
Industries that extract and use natural resources, such as farming, fishing, and mining.
Industries that manufacture goods using raw materials from the primary sector.
Industries that provide services to consumers and other businesses.
Industries that provide knowledge-based services such as research, ICT, and consultancy.
An economy with a combination of private and public sector ownership and control.
An economic system where the government makes all decisions about resource allocation.
An economy where decisions on production and prices are determined by supply and demand with minimal government intervention.
The part of the economy owned and operated by individuals and businesses for profit.
The part of the economy owned and controlled by the government.
The process of growing the manufacturing sector of an economy.
The decline of the manufacturing sector in an economy.
A document outlining a business’s objectives, strategies, target market, and financial forecasts.
The total value of capital used for the acquisition of profits in a business.
Business expansion achieved through increasing sales and output rather than through mergers or takeovers.
Business expansion achieved by merging with or acquiring other businesses.
When one business acquires control of another business by buying its shares.
When two businesses join together to form one new business.
When two businesses in the same industry and at the same stage of production merge or are acquired.
When a business merges with or takes over another business in the next stage of production.
When a business merges with or takes over a supplier at an earlier stage of production.
When a business merges with or acquires another in a completely unrelated industry.
A growth strategy involving entry into new markets with new products.
A business owned and run by one person with unlimited liability.
A business owned by two or more people who share profits and responsibilities.
A company whose shares are privately held and not available to the public.
A company whose shares are traded on the stock exchange and available to the public.
Organizations owned and run by members who share the benefits.
A share of the company’s profits paid to shareholders.
A yearly meeting of shareholders to discuss company performance and elect directors.
A document that defines the responsibilities of directors and the handling of the company’s affairs.
A document outlining a company’s structure and purpose, required for incorporation.
An agreement where one business allows another to trade under its name and sell its products.
A person or business that buys the rights to operate under a franchise’s brand.
A business that grants a franchise to others.
A payment made to the franchisor based on sales revenue.
A business arrangement where two or more parties agree to work together on a project.
Organizations owned and run by the government.
Businesses with a separate legal identity from their owners.
Businesses where there is no legal distinction between the owner and the business.
Specific, measurable goals that a business aims to achieve.
A business that aims to achieve social objectives as well as making a profit.
People within a business who have an interest in its performance, such as employees and managers.
Individuals or groups outside a business affected by its operations, such as suppliers and the community.
When the interests of different stakeholders are in opposition.

People in business.definition: The internal and external factors that stimulate desire and energy in people to be continually interested in and committed to a job or role.
A motivation theory suggesting that human needs are arranged in a hierarchy, from basic physiological needs to self-actualization.
The realization of one's full potential and personal growth after all other needs are satisfied.
The need for self-respect, recognition, and respect from others.
The human requirement for love, friendship, and belonging.
The need for personal safety, financial security, health, and protection from harm.
Basic human needs for survival, such as food, water, shelter, and sleep.
Payment to a worker, usually calculated by the hour or day, based on the time worked.
Factors that increase job satisfaction and encourage greater effort, such as achievement and recognition.
Workplace conditions that prevent dissatisfaction but do not necessarily motivate, such as salary and work environment.
A theory that workers are primarily motivated by money.
Payment based on the amount of time worked.
Payment based on the number of units produced.
A fixed regular payment, typically paid monthly, not dependent on hours worked.
An additional payment given to employees as a reward for performance.
Payment to a salesperson based on the value or number of sales made.
A system in which employees receive a share of the company’s profits.
The practice of moving employees between different tasks to increase variety and skills.
A group of employees working collaboratively to achieve a common goal.
The process of developing employees' skills and knowledge to improve performance.
Advancing an employee to a higher-level position within the organization.
Increasing the range of tasks performed by an employee to reduce monotony.
Increasing job depth by giving employees more responsibility and autonomy.
Altering the structure of a job to make it more motivating and efficient.
The way in which the roles, responsibilities, and authority are arranged in an organization.
A diagram showing the internal structure of an organization.
A system in which members of an organization are ranked according to their level of authority.
A layer of management within an organization.
The line of authority within an organization.
The number of subordinates a manager is directly responsible for.
Managers who have direct responsibility over subordinates.
Employees who oversee and guide the work of other employees.
Managers who support line managers by providing advice and services.
Assigning responsibility and authority to a subordinate to carry out specific activities.
Different approaches to leading and managing people.
A leadership style where the leader makes decisions without input from others.
A leadership style where the leader involves team members in decision-making.
A leadership style where the leader gives employees freedom to make decisions.
An organization of workers formed to protect and promote their rights and interests.
Negotiations between employers and trade unions over pay and working conditions.
Measures taken by employees, such as strikes, to enforce demands.
A workplace where all employees must be members of a trade union.
The process of attracting suitable candidates for job positions.
The process of choosing the most suitable candidate for a job.
The process of gathering information about the duties and skills required for a job.
A detailed outline of the duties, responsibilities, and reporting relationships of a job.
A statement of the qualifications, skills, and experience required for a job.
Filling job vacancies with existing employees from within the organization.
Filling job vacancies with candidates from outside the organization.
An employee who works fewer hours than a full-time employee.
An employee who works the standard number of hours set by the employer.
Training given to new employees to introduce them to the organization and their role.
Training conducted while the employee is performing their job.
Training away from the workplace, such as in a classroom or training center.
Ensuring the right number of employees with the right skills are available when needed.
When an employer ends an employee’s contract due to poor performance or misconduct.
Dismissal based on valid reasons such as misconduct or redundancy.
Dismissal without a fair reason or without following proper procedures.
When a job is no longer needed and the employee is let go.
A legal agreement between an employer and employee outlining terms and conditions of employment.
A legal body that resolves disputes between employers and employees.
Choices made based on moral principles and values.
The process of transferring information from one person to another.
The information or content being communicated.
Communication between people within the same organization.
Communication between an organization and people outside it.
Communication where information flows only in one direction without feedback.
Communication where the receiver can provide feedback to the sender.
Official, structured communication following the organization’s hierarchy.
Unstructured communication that takes place outside official channels.
Obstacles that prevent effective communication.
Specialized terms used by a particular profession or group that may not be understood by others.

Marketing.definition: The process of identifying, anticipating, and satisfying customer needs profitably.
An individual or business that purchases goods or services from a company.
The tendency of customers to continue buying from the same brand over time.
The strategies and technologies businesses use to manage and analyze customer interactions to improve relationships and retention.
The percentage of total sales in a market accounted for by one business or product.
A person who uses goods and services for personal consumption.
Selling the same product to a large market with no attempt to target specific groups.
Targeting a specific, well-defined segment of the market.
A group of consumers with similar characteristics or needs.
Dividing a market into distinct groups of consumers with similar needs or characteristics.
Dividing the market based on location such as country, region, or city.
Dividing the market based on lifestyle, values, attitudes, and personality.
Dividing the market based on characteristics such as age, gender, income, or education.
The process of gathering and analyzing information about consumers’ needs and preferences.
The collection of new, first-hand information directly from sources such as surveys or interviews.
Using existing data collected by others, such as reports or statistics.
A business approach focused on product quality and innovation rather than customer needs.
A business approach that prioritizes identifying and meeting customer needs.
The amount of money a business allocates to marketing activities over a set period.
A set of written questions used to collect information from respondents.
A method of collecting data from respondents via the internet.
A method of gathering information through face-to-face, telephone, or online conversations.
A small group of people brought together to discuss their views about a product or service.
Selecting a portion of the population to represent the whole for research purposes.
A sampling method where every individual in the population has an equal chance of being selected.
A sampling method where researchers select participants to fit specific characteristics.
The combination of Product, Price, Place, and Promotion used to market a product.
A feature or benefit that makes a product different from and better than competitors’ products.
The name given to a product or range of products that distinguishes it from competitors.
The commitment of consumers to repurchase a brand's products consistently.
The perception of a brand held in consumers’ minds.
The design and production of containers or wrappers for a product.
The stages a product goes through: introduction, growth, maturity, and decline.
Methods used to prolong the maturity stage of a product’s life, such as updates or new markets.
The stage in the product lifecycle where sales growth slows or levels off.
When the market for a product is fully supplied and sales growth stops.
Setting a price by adding a fixed percentage to the cost of producing a product.
Setting a price based on what competitors are charging.
Setting a low price to enter a market and attract customers quickly.
Setting a high price initially and lowering it over time to attract different customer segments.
Temporarily reducing the price to increase short-term sales.
Adjusting prices based on current market demand and supply conditions.
A measure of how sensitive quantity demanded is to a change in price.
When a small change in price causes a large change in quantity demanded.
When a change in price has little effect on quantity demanded.
The route a product takes from the producer to the consumer.
A business or person that helps distribute products from producer to consumer.
An intermediary who negotiates sales on behalf of a producer.
Activities to inform and persuade customers to buy a product.
Paid communication intended to inform, persuade, or remind customers about products.
Advertising designed to give information about a product’s features and benefits.
Advertising aimed at encouraging customers to buy by appealing to emotions or desires.
The specific group of consumers a marketing campaign is aimed at.
Short-term incentives to encourage purchases, such as discounts or free samples.
A sales promotion where customers buy one product and get another free.
Activities aimed at creating and maintaining a positive image of a business.
Financial support for an event, activity, or organization in return for publicity.
Including a product in media content as a form of advertising.
Using social media platforms to promote products and engage with customers.
Encouraging customers to share a marketing message to spread it rapidly.
Buying and selling goods or services over the internet.
The goals a business aims to achieve through its marketing efforts.
A plan of action for promoting and selling products to achieve marketing objectives.

Operations Management.definition: A measure of the output produced per unit of input, such as per worker or per hour.
Producing goods and services using the least amount of resources without waste.
A reserve of goods kept to ensure supply in case of unexpected demand or delays.
A production method focused on minimizing waste while maintaining quality.
A production strategy where materials and components arrive exactly when needed to reduce inventory costs.
A philosophy of continuous improvement involving all employees.
Producing custom goods one at a time to meet specific customer requirements.
Producing a group of identical products together in a batch before moving to the next batch.
Producing goods in a continuous process along a production line.
Computer-Aided Design – using computers to create, modify, and test product designs.
Computer-Aided Manufacturing – using computers to control and manage production machinery.
The level of sales at which total revenue equals total costs, resulting in no profit or loss.
The difference between actual sales and the breakeven level of sales.
A cost that does not change with the level of output.
A cost that changes directly with the level of output.
Selling price per unit minus variable cost per unit; used to cover fixed costs and profit.
Cost advantages that a business experiences as it increases production.
Cost savings from buying raw materials in bulk at lower prices.
Lower marketing costs per unit when spreading costs over larger output.
Access to finance at lower interest rates due to larger size and stability.
Specialist managers improve efficiency, reducing costs per unit.
Cost savings from using more efficient, specialized production equipment.
Rising average costs when a business becomes too large and inefficient.
The standard of a product measured against set requirements or customer expectations.
The process of checking products to ensure they meet required standards.
A system ensuring quality is maintained throughout the production process.
A company-wide approach to continuous quality improvement, involving all employees.

Financial Information and Decisions.definition: The initial money needed to start a business, covering expenses before revenue is generated.
The difference between current assets and current liabilities, showing short-term financial health.
Spending on fixed assets such as buildings, machinery, or vehicles.
Spending on day-to-day operating costs such as wages and utilities.
Money raised from within the business, such as retained profits or asset sales.
Money raised from outside the business, such as bank loans or share issues.
Small loans provided to entrepreneurs in developing countries who lack access to traditional banking.
Raising finance by collecting small amounts of money from many people, often via the internet.
Selling accounts receivable to a third party to get immediate cash.
Profits kept in the business instead of being paid out as dividends.
A type of long-term loan to a business, usually secured against assets.
Buying an asset by paying in installments while using it; ownership passes after final payment.
Money borrowed from a bank, to be repaid with interest over an agreed period.
A contract allowing the use of an asset without owning it, in exchange for regular payments.
An arrangement allowing a business to buy goods and pay for them later.
Non-repayable funds provided by the government to support businesses or projects.
Investment from individuals or firms in exchange for equity in high-risk businesses.
Selling shares of the company to raise capital.
Money coming into a business from sales, investments, or financing.
Money leaving a business for expenses, purchases, or repayments.
The time between paying for goods and receiving cash from their sale.
An estimate of future cash inflows and outflows over a period.
The difference between total cash inflows and total cash outflows in a period.
Financial records summarizing a business’s transactions and performance.

Financial Information and Decisions.term: Income Statement / Statement of Profit and Loss
Financial Information and Decisions.definition: A financial report showing revenue, expenses, and profit over a period.

Financial Information and Decisions.term: Balance Sheet / Statement of Financial Position
Financial Information and Decisions.definition: A snapshot of a company’s assets, liabilities, and equity at a specific point in time.
The total cost of producing goods sold during a period.
Revenue minus cost of sales.
Costs incurred in running the business excluding cost of sales.
The profit remaining after all expenses, including taxes and interest, are deducted.
The cost of borrowing money, such as interest payments.
The reduction in value of an asset over time due to wear and tear.
The portion of net profits kept in the business after dividends are paid.
Part of the income statement showing sales, cost of sales, and gross profit.
Resources owned by a business that have economic value.
Assets kept for long-term use, such as buildings and machinery.
Assets expected to be converted into cash within a year.
Debts or obligations owed by a business.
Debts due for repayment after more than one year.
Debts due for repayment within one year.
The value of ownership interest in the business.
Money invested in a company by shareholders in exchange for shares.
The total value of all long-term funds invested in a business.
The ability of a business to meet its short-term financial obligations.
A liquidity ratio comparing current assets to current liabilities.
A liquidity ratio comparing quick assets to current liabilities, excluding inventory.
When a business does not have enough liquid assets to meet its short-term debts.
The ability of a business to generate profit.
Net profit as a percentage of revenue.
Gross profit as a percentage of revenue.
Return on Capital Employed – a measure of profitability relative to capital invested.

External Influences on Business Activity.definition: The total value of all goods and services produced in a country over a period.
A period of strong economic growth and high levels of activity.
An increase in the output of goods and services in an economy over time.
A period of negative economic growth lasting at least two consecutive quarters.
A prolonged period of economic stagnation and low activity.
A sustained increase in the general price level of goods and services.
A sustained decrease in the general price level of goods and services.
The situation where people who are willing and able to work cannot find jobs.
The condition where all available labour resources are being used efficiently.
An increase in a country's production of goods and services over time.
A record of all economic transactions between a country and the rest of the world.
When a country exports more goods and services than it imports.
When a country imports more goods and services than it exports.
A fall in the value of a country's currency relative to other currencies.
An increase in the value of a country's currency relative to other currencies.
Government policy on taxation and spending to influence the economy.
Government or central bank policy on money supply and interest rates.
Government measures to increase the productive capacity of the economy.
The transfer of ownership of a business from the public sector to the private sector.
The transfer of ownership of a business from the private sector to the public sector.
Taxes paid directly to the government by individuals or businesses, such as income tax.
Taxes levied on goods and services, such as VAT or sales tax.
Financial assistance given by the government to support certain industries or activities.
The income left after paying taxes, available for spending and saving.
Income adjusted for inflation, showing the purchasing power of earnings.
Taxes on imported goods to make them more expensive and protect domestic industries.
A limit on the quantity of a product that can be imported.
The obligation of businesses to act in ways that benefit society.
Organizations that try to influence business or government policies.
Costs or benefits of an activity experienced by third parties who are not directly involved.
The cost of an activity borne by the individual or business undertaking it.
The benefits of an activity enjoyed by the individual or business undertaking it.
Costs imposed on third parties as a result of an activity.
Benefits enjoyed by third parties as a result of an activity.
The total cost to society of an activity, including both private and external costs.
The total benefit to society of an activity, including both private and external benefits.
Development that meets current needs without harming the ability of future generations to meet theirs.
The increasing interconnectedness of economies, cultures, and societies worldwide.
Refusing to buy products from a company to protest against its policies or practices.
Choices made based on moral principles and what is considered right or wrong.

The basic economic problem.definition: Natural resources used in production, such as minerals, water, forests, and land area.
Human effort, both physical and mental, used in producing goods and services.
Man-made resources such as machinery, tools, and buildings used in production.
The ability and willingness to take risks and organize other factors of production to create goods or services.
The condition where resources are limited but wants are unlimited.
Basic requirements essential for survival, such as food, water, and shelter.
Desires for goods and services that improve quality of life but are not essential for survival.
Goods that are scarce and have an opportunity cost.
Goods that are abundant and have no opportunity cost, such as air.
Individuals who take risks and organize the factors of production to start and run businesses.
The ease with which workers can move between different jobs or locations.
The total number of people available and willing to work in an economy.
The ability of workers to move to different locations for employment.
The ability of workers to change between different types of jobs.
The amount of output produced per unit of input.
The amount of output produced per worker over a period of time.
The total amount of goods or services produced.
The total amount spent on new capital goods, including replacement of worn-out capital.
The loss in value of capital goods over time due to wear and tear.
Gross investment minus depreciation.
The next best alternative forgone when a choice is made.
A situation where choosing more of one thing means having less of another.
A diagram showing the maximum possible output combinations of two goods given available resources and technology.
Represents efficient use of resources, producing at maximum capacity.
Represents inefficient use of resources or underutilization.
Represents an output combination that is currently unattainable with available resources.
When the opportunity cost of producing one good in terms of another remains the same.
As production of one good increases, the opportunity cost of producing additional units rises.

The Allocation of Resources.definition: A place or system where buyers and sellers exchange goods and services.
Individuals or groups who buy goods and services for personal use.
Businesses that produce and sell goods or services.
Natural resources used to produce goods.
The willingness and ability to buy a good or service at a given price.
Demand backed by the ability to pay.
Desire for a product without the ability to pay.
When price falls, quantity demanded rises, and when price rises, quantity demanded falls, ceteris paribus.
The total quantity demanded by all consumers in a market.
An increase in quantity demanded due to a fall in price.
A decrease in quantity demanded due to a rise in price.
An increase in demand at every price level.
A decrease in demand at every price level.
Goods for which demand increases as income increases.
Goods for which demand decreases as income increases.
Goods that can replace each other in consumption.
Goods that are consumed together.
The willingness and ability of producers to sell goods or services at a given price.
Supply without the ability to actually sell the good or service.
Supply backed by the ability to produce and sell.
When price rises, quantity supplied increases, and when price falls, quantity supplied decreases, ceteris paribus.
The total quantity supplied by all producers in a market.
The quantity supplied by a single producer.
An increase in supply at every price level.
A decrease in supply at every price level.
An increase in quantity supplied due to a rise in price.
A decrease in quantity supplied due to a fall in price.
A charge imposed by the government on individuals or businesses.
A tax paid directly to the government, such as income tax.
A tax levied on goods and services, such as VAT.
A payment made by the government to encourage production or consumption of certain goods.
The point where demand equals supply in a market.
When demand and supply are not equal.
When quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied.
When quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded.
The way prices allocate resources through changes in demand and supply.
Allocating resources, signalling information, and rationing goods.
A measure of how responsive demand is to changes in price.
When demand changes by a larger percentage than the change in price.
When demand changes by a smaller percentage than the change in price.
When demand changes by the same percentage as the change in price.
When demand does not change at all with a change in price.
When demand changes infinitely with any change in price.
Products with close substitutes, leading to more elastic demand.
Products with few substitutes, leading to more inelastic demand.
A measure of how responsive supply is to changes in price.
When supply changes by a larger percentage than the change in price.
When supply changes by a smaller percentage than the change in price.
When supply changes by the same percentage as the change in price.
When supply does not change at all with a change in price.
When supply changes infinitely with any change in price.
An economic system where the government makes all production decisions.
An economic system where decisions are driven by supply and demand.
An economy with both government and market decision-making.
Businesses owned and operated by the government.
Orders or instructions issued, often by the government, to control economic activity.
The part of the economy owned by the government.
The part of the economy owned by individuals and businesses.
Industries that extract natural resources.
Industries that process raw materials into finished goods.
Industries that provide services.
Industries that provide knowledge-based services.
Transfer of ownership from the public sector to the private sector.
Transfer of ownership from the private sector to the public sector.
A person who benefits from a good without paying for it.
When markets fail to allocate resources efficiently.
Goods that provide benefits to society and are often under-consumed.
Goods that have negative effects on society and are often over-consumed.
Goods that are non-excludable and non-rivalrous.
When people cannot be prevented from using a good.
When one person's use of a good does not reduce its availability to others.
When people benefit from a good without contributing to its cost.
Costs borne by the producer or consumer directly involved in a transaction.
Benefits enjoyed by the producer or consumer directly involved in a transaction.
Costs imposed on third parties by economic activities.
Benefits gained by third parties from economic activities.
The total of private and external costs.
The total of private and external benefits.
Social costs minus social benefits.
Social benefits minus social costs.
The level of output where social benefits equal social costs.
When buyers or sellers lack full information, leading to poor decisions.
A market with a single seller dominating supply.
When firms agree to set prices rather than competing.
A maximum price set by the government.
A minimum price set by the government.
Illegal buying and selling of goods and services.
Focusing on immediate gains at the expense of long-term performance.
Rules set by the government to control business practices.
Industries important for a country's economy, security, or infrastructure.
Markets where a single firm can supply the entire market more efficiently than multiple firms.

The Allocation of Resources.term: National Champions: Industries that are, or have potential to be world leaders.
The Allocation of Resources.definition: Key domestic industries supported to become global leaders.
Limits on the quantity of goods that can be imported or produced.

Microeconomics decision-makers.definition: An individual who purchases goods and services for personal use.
A person or organization that makes goods or provides services.
The quantity of a good or service consumers are willing and able to buy at different prices.
The quantity of a good or service producers are willing and able to provide at different prices.
A place or system where buyers and sellers interact to exchange goods and services.

Government and Macroeconomy.definition: Gross Domestic Product - the total value of goods and services produced in a country.
A sustained increase in the general price level of goods and services.
The situation where people willing and able to work cannot find employment.
Government policy regarding taxation and spending to influence the economy.
Central bank policy regarding money supply and interest rates to control inflation and economic growth.

Economic Development.definition: An increase in a country's real GDP over time.
The level of wealth, comfort, and material goods available to people.
A country with low levels of industrialization and income per capita.
A country with high levels of industrialization and income per capita.
A measure combining life expectancy, education, and income indicators.

International trade and globalization.definition: The increasing integration of economies, cultures, and societies worldwide.
Trade between countries without restrictions such as tariffs or quotas.
Government policies designed to protect domestic industries from foreign competition.
A country's ability to produce a good at a lower opportunity cost than another country.
When a country exports more than it imports.

Business and Its Environment.definition: Any individual or group that has an interest in or is affected by a business's activities.
A business's commitment to contribute to sustainable economic development.
The moral principles that guide business behavior and decision-making.
The effect that business activities have on the natural environment.
Meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet their needs.

Human Resource Management.definition: The process of attracting and selecting suitable candidates for job positions.
A systematic evaluation of employee performance and potential.
The level of contentment employees feel with their work.
The assignment of responsibility and authority to subordinates.
The process of ensuring the right people are in the right jobs at the right time.

Marketing.definition: Dividing a market into distinct groups of consumers with similar characteristics.
The stages a product goes through from introduction to withdrawal from the market.
The tendency of customers to repeatedly purchase from the same brand.
The percentage of total market sales captured by a particular company or product.
The study of how individuals and groups select, purchase, and use products.

Operations Management.definition: The percentage of total production capacity that is actually being used.
A production strategy where materials arrive exactly when needed in the production process.
A management approach focused on continuous improvement and customer satisfaction.
A measure of output per unit of input over time.
Contracting business processes to external service providers.

Finance.definition: The short-term assets minus short-term liabilities of a business.
The proportion of a company's capital that is borrowed money.
The ability of a business to meet its short-term financial obligations.
A measure of the efficiency of an investment.
The level of sales at which total costs equal total revenue.

Basic Economic Ideas and Resource Alloca.definition: A curve showing the maximum combination of goods that can be produced with available resources.
The optimal distribution of resources to maximize output and minimize waste.
The concentration of production on a limited range of goods and services.
The assignment of different tasks to different workers in the production process.
The sacrifice of one thing in order to gain another.

The price system and microeconomy.definition: The responsiveness of quantity demanded to changes in price.
The difference between what consumers are willing to pay and what they actually pay.
The difference between the price producers receive and their minimum acceptable price.
When the free market fails to allocate resources efficiently.
The point where quantity demanded equals quantity supplied.

Government Microeconomic Intervention.definition: Government regulations that set maximum or minimum prices for goods and services.
Financial assistance given by the government to support specific industries or activities.
A compulsory payment to the government based on income, property, or transactions.
Rules and laws set by government to control business behavior.
A good that is non-excludable and non-rivalrous in consumption.

The macroeconomy.definition: The total demand for goods and services in an economy at different price levels.
The total supply of goods and services in an economy at different price levels.
The periodic fluctuations in economic activity characterized by expansion and contraction.
Statistics used to measure the performance of an economy.
The level of employment where only frictional and structural unemployment exist.

Government Macroeconomic Intervention.definition: When government spending exceeds government revenue in a given period.
The total amount of money owed by the government.
A monetary policy where central banks purchase securities to increase money supply.
Government policies aimed at increasing the productive capacity of the economy.
Economic policies that automatically adjust government spending or taxes during economic fluctuations.

International Economic Issues.definition: A record of all economic transactions between a country and the rest of the world.
The part of balance of payments recording trade in goods, services, and transfers.
Investment by companies in productive assets in foreign countries.
An increase in the value of one currency relative to other currencies.
When a country imports more than it exports.

External Influences on Business Activity.definition: The characteristics of a market that determine the behavior of firms within it.
A market structure where there is only one seller of a product with no close substitutes.
A market structure dominated by a small number of large firms.
A market structure with many buyers and sellers, identical products, and free entry/exit.
Obstacles that prevent new firms from entering a market.

Strategic Business Management.definition: The process of defining organizational direction and making decisions about resource allocation.
A strategic planning tool examining Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats.
A unique capability that gives a company competitive advantage.
A growth strategy involving expansion into new markets or products.
A partnership between companies to achieve mutual strategic objectives.

Human Resource Management.definition: The process of helping organizations transition from current to desired future states.
The shared values, beliefs, and practices within an organization.
Giving employees authority and responsibility to make decisions about their work.
The strategic management of human capital to optimize business performance.
The process of identifying and developing future leaders within an organization.

Marketing.definition: Marketing strategy that treats the world as a single market.
Marketing using digital channels and technologies to reach consumers.
Strategies and technologies for managing customer interactions and data.
The unique value a company promises to deliver to customers.
A strategy to increase market share within existing market segments.

Operations Management.definition: The coordination of production, shipment, and distribution of products.
A quality management methodology focused on reducing defects and variation.
A Japanese philosophy of continuous improvement in all aspects of business.
The practice of comparing business processes with industry best practices.
The series of activities that create and deliver value to customers.

Finance.definition: The mix of debt and equity financing used by a company.
The cost of obtaining funds through debt or equity financing.
The possibility of financial loss due to business or market conditions.
The evaluation of potential investments to determine their profitability.
The system of rules and practices by which companies are directed and controlled.

Theory of Consumer Behavior.definition: The additional satisfaction or benefit gained from consuming one more unit of a good or service. Marginal utility typically decreases as more units are consumed, reflecting the Law of Diminishing Marginal Utility. For example, eating the first slice of pizza gives more enjoyment than the fourth or fifth slice.

The total satisfaction received from consuming a certain quantity of goods or services. It is the sum of the marginal utilities of each unit consumed. For instance, if each chocolate bar gives some enjoyment, the total utility is the combined enjoyment from all bars eaten.

A principle stating that as a person consumes more units of a particular good, the additional satisfaction from each new unit tends to decrease. This explains why demand curves typically slope downwards.

The rule that consumers maximize their total utility when the marginal utility per unit of currency spent is equal across all goods and services purchased. This principle guides rational allocation of limited income among competing goods.

An economic concept that assumes utility can be measured and expressed in numerical units, allowing direct comparison between the satisfaction gained from different goods. For example, a consumer might say an apple gives 10 utils of satisfaction and a banana gives 8 utils.

An approach to utility measurement that assumes satisfaction cannot be measured in exact numbers but can be ranked in order of preference. For example, a consumer might prefer tea to coffee, and coffee to juice, without specifying by how much.
A decision-maker who allocates income to maximize total satisfaction, making choices based on marginal utility, prices, and budget constraints.
The additional satisfaction gained from spending one more unit of currency on a good or service. It is calculated by dividing the marginal utility of a good by its price.

An observation that some goods essential for survival (like water) often have low market prices, while non-essential goods (like diamonds) may have high prices. This is explained by differences in marginal utility and scarcity.
The total utility from all units consumed divided by the number of units. It provides an idea of the average satisfaction per unit.

A graph showing different combinations of two goods that give the consumer the same level of satisfaction. Along an indifference curve, the consumer has no preference for one combination over another.
A collection of indifference curves representing a consumer’s preferences over a range of goods. Higher curves represent higher levels of satisfaction.
The rate at which a consumer is willing to give up units of one good in exchange for an additional unit of another good while maintaining the same level of satisfaction.
A graph showing all possible combinations of two goods a consumer can buy given their income and the prices of the goods. It represents the consumer’s spending constraint.

Theory of Consumer Behavior.term: Law of Diminishing Marginal Rate of Substitution
Theory of Consumer Behavior.definition: A principle stating that as a consumer substitutes one good for another, the willingness to give up units of the first good to obtain an additional unit of the second good decreases.
The point at which a consumer has allocated their income in a way that maximizes total satisfaction, given prices and budget constraints.
Goods that can replace each other completely in consumption, providing the same level of satisfaction. For example, two brands of bottled water.
Goods that are consumed together in fixed proportions to maximize satisfaction, such as left shoes and right shoes.
A graph showing how the quantity of a good consumed changes as the consumer’s income changes, holding prices constant.
The total effect of a change in the price of a good on the quantity demanded, composed of both the substitution effect and the income effect.
The change in quantity demanded of a good resulting from a change in the consumer’s real income or purchasing power.
The change in quantity demanded due to a change in the relative price of goods, making one good more or less attractive compared to others.
Represents the rate at which one good can be substituted for another without changing total expenditure. It equals the negative ratio of the prices of the two goods.
The property that indifference curves are bowed towards the origin, reflecting a consumer’s preference for balanced consumption bundles over extremes.
The principle that if a consumer prefers good A to good B, and good B to good C, then they will prefer good A to good C, ensuring consistency in choices.
The point of maximum satisfaction on an indifference map, beyond which any change in consumption would reduce satisfaction.
A type of inferior good for which an increase in price leads to an increase in quantity demanded due to a strong negative income effect outweighing the substitution effect.
Goods for which demand decreases as consumer income rises, such as instant noodles for higher-income consumers.
The assumption that more of a good is always preferred to less, holding other factors constant.
A rule stating that indifference curves cannot intersect, as this would imply contradictory preferences.

Economic Efficiency and Market Failure.definition: Producing a given level of output at the lowest possible average cost with current technology and inputs. In diagram terms, firms operate at the minimum point of the long-run average cost curve or on the production possibility frontier. Waste is minimized and resources are fully utilized.

Resources are allocated to produce the mix of goods and services most valued by society, where price equals marginal cost (P = MC). At this point, the last unit produced yields a marginal benefit equal to its marginal resource cost, so no reallocation can improve welfare. It is a key benchmark for well-functioning competitive markets.

Efficiency achieved over time through innovation, R&D, learning-by-doing, and investment that reduce costs or improve product quality. It reflects how quickly and effectively firms adapt technologies and processes. Market structures with healthy competitive pressure and the expectation of future profits tend to promote dynamic efficiency.

An allocation of resources where no one can be made better off without making someone else worse off. It is a strict efficiency criterion that says nothing about fairness or equality. Many Pareto-efficient outcomes can exist, including ones with very unequal distributions.

When firms produce above the minimum possible cost due to weak competition, managerial slack, or poor incentives. Costs are higher than technically necessary even with the same technology. It is often associated with monopolies or heavily protected industries.

Producing the maximum feasible output from a given set of inputs, or using the minimum inputs for a given output, given current technology. Technically inefficient firms lie inside the production possibility frontier. It focuses on physical input–output relationships, not prices.

The extra cost to society from producing one more unit of a good, including both private production costs and any external costs imposed on third parties. Formally, MSC = MPC + MEC. In the presence of negative externalities, MSC exceeds private marginal cost.

The additional benefit to society from consuming one more unit of a good, including the private benefit to buyers plus any positive spillovers to others. Formally, MSB = MPB + MEB. With positive externalities, MSB exceeds private marginal benefit.

The extra cost to the producer of making one more unit, excluding any external costs. It is the cost relevant to a firm’s supply decision in the absence of regulation. In competitive markets without externalities, MPC equals MSC.
The additional benefit to the individual consumer from one extra unit consumed, reflected in the demand curve. It excludes any spillover benefits to others. Without externalities, MPB equals MSB.

The additional cost imposed on third parties when one more unit is produced or consumed. Examples include pollution, congestion, or noise. When MEC > 0, unregulated markets overproduce relative to the social optimum.

The extra benefit enjoyed by third parties when one more unit is consumed or produced. Examples include herd immunity from vaccination or knowledge spillovers from R&D. When MEB > 0, markets underprovide the good without intervention.

The difference between total social costs and total social benefits for an activity when costs exceed benefits (NSC = TSC − TSB > 0). A positive net social cost indicates a welfare loss relative to doing less of the activity. Policymakers aim to reduce activities with high net social costs.

The excess of total social benefits over total social costs (NSB = TSB − TSC). The socially efficient policy or output is the one that maximizes NSB. On MSC–MSB diagrams, this occurs where MSC equals MSB.

Spillover costs from the production process borne by third parties, such as pollution from factories or environmental damage from resource extraction. The private supply curve (MPC) lies below the social cost curve (MSC), causing overproduction and a deadweight welfare loss. Corrective tools include Pigouvian taxes, regulation, and tradable permits.

Spillover costs from consumption that harm third parties, such as second-hand smoke, noise, or drunk driving risks. Private demand (MPB) exceeds social benefit (MSB), leading to overconsumption. Policy responses include excise taxes, age limits, bans in public spaces, and information campaigns.

Spillover benefits to others from a person’s consumption, such as vaccination, education, or healthy behaviors that reduce public health costs. MSB lies above MPB, so the market underconsumes. Subsidies, vouchers, or public provision can raise consumption toward the social optimum.

Spillover benefits created during production, like knowledge diffusion from R&D or training that raises workers’ future productivity. MSC lies below MPC (or equivalently MSB > MPB), so the market underproduces. Policy tools include R&D tax credits, patents, and targeted subsidies.

A situation where the free market, left alone, does not allocate resources efficiently, resulting in welfare loss. Common causes include externalities, public goods, market power, information failures, and factor immobility. Well-designed government policies can sometimes improve outcomes.

Goods that generate private and external benefits but are underconsumed in free markets due to information gaps or myopia (e.g., education, vaccinations). Governments encourage consumption via subsidies, free provision, or information campaigns. The aim is to move consumption closer to where MSB = MSC.

Goods that create private and external costs and tend to be overconsumed (e.g., tobacco, harmful drugs). Consumers may underestimate risks or addiction. Policy responses include taxes, age restrictions, advertising bans, and warnings.

Once provided, it is difficult or costly to prevent non-payers from using the good. This leads to free-riding and underprovision by private markets. Classic examples are street lighting and national defense.
One person’s use of the good does not diminish the amount available for others. The marginal cost of serving an extra user is near zero. Pure public goods are both non-rivalrous and non-excludable.

When individuals benefit from a good without paying, relying on others to fund provision. This undermines market incentives to produce public goods. Collective funding (e.g., taxation) or excludability mechanisms can address it.

Goods that are both non-excludable and non-rivalrous, such as national defense or lighthouse services. Private markets underprovide them because producers cannot easily charge users. Governments often finance provision through taxation.

Goods that are partially excludable or become rivalrous beyond a capacity threshold (e.g., uncongested roads are non-rival, but congestion creates rivalry). Smart pricing or access control can improve efficiency. They lie between pure private and pure public goods.

Taxes levied on unexpectedly high or ‘windfall’ profits, often in sectors like energy or commodities. Intended to capture extraordinary gains not due to firms’ own effort or investment. Critics warn about discouraging future investment if used unpredictably.

A behavioral economics approach that alters ‘choice architecture’ to steer people toward better decisions without restricting options or significantly changing incentives. Examples include default enrollment in pensions or opt-out organ donation. Nudges are low-cost and preserve freedom of choice.

A market dominated by a single firm with significant entry barriers, allowing price-making power. Output is typically restricted and prices elevated relative to competitive levels, creating deadweight loss. Remedies include regulation, antitrust action, or fostering entry.

Setting price equal to marginal cost (P = MC) to achieve allocative efficiency. Under natural monopoly, P = MC may be below average cost, requiring a subsidy to avoid losses. Regulators sometimes use average-cost or price-cap rules as practical alternatives.

The output level where marginal social benefit equals marginal social cost (MSB = MSC). At this point, net social benefit is maximized and no further welfare gains are possible by changing output. Externalities shift the MSB or MSC curves and move the optimal output away from the private market equilibrium.

When public intervention intended to correct market failure leads to a worse allocation of resources. Causes include information problems, regulatory capture, political incentives, administrative costs, and unintended consequences. Effective policy design requires humility, evaluation, and sunset clauses.

When buyers or sellers lack accurate, timely, or comprehensible information to make good decisions. It includes uncertainty, complexity, and bounded rationality. Consequences range from under- or over-consumption to mispriced risk.

A form of information failure where one party to a transaction knows more than the other (e.g., sellers know the quality of used cars better than buyers). It can lead to market collapse or misallocation. Solutions include disclosure rules, warranties, signaling, and screening.

When a party is protected from the consequences of risk and therefore behaves more riskily after a contract is signed (e.g., insured drivers being less careful). It arises from hidden actions. Deductibles, co-payments, and monitoring help mitigate it.

When hidden information before contracting leads to a disproportionate share of high-risk or low-quality entrants (e.g., sicker people more likely to buy health insurance). Markets may unravel as average quality falls. Screening and risk-based pricing are common remedies.

A conflict of interest and information between a principal (owner) and an agent (manager) whose actions are hard to observe. Agents may pursue their own goals rather than the principal’s. Incentive contracts, performance pay, and governance checks align interests.

A corrective tax set equal to the marginal external cost at the socially optimal output, shifting private incentives to internalize the externality. It raises the private marginal cost so that MPC + tax ≈ MSC. Examples include carbon taxes and congestion charges.

A subsidy set equal to the marginal external benefit to encourage activities with positive spillovers. It raises private marginal benefit so that MPB + subsidy ≈ MSB. Examples include R&D tax credits and vaccination subsidies.

An environmental policy that sets an overall emissions ‘cap’ and issues tradable permits equal to that cap. Firms that reduce emissions cheaply sell permits to those facing higher abatement costs, equalizing marginal abatement costs across firms. The cap guarantees the environmental outcome while trading minimizes total compliance cost.

An individual or group not directly involved in a transaction who is nonetheless affected by it. Third parties bear external costs (e.g., pollution harm) or reap external benefits (e.g., herd immunity). Their interests are central to externality analysis.

A direct participant in a transaction or activity—typically the decision-maker such as the buyer/consumer or producer/seller. Their private costs and benefits are captured by MPC and MPB. Policy seeks to align first-party incentives with social outcomes.

The counterparty to the first party in a transaction (e.g., buyer vs. seller). Together, first and second parties internalize private costs and benefits but not externalities. Contracts and market prices reflect their private information and incentives.

Rights to emit a specified amount of a pollutant that firms can buy and sell. Under a cap-and-trade system, total permits equal the emissions cap; trading directs abatement to the lowest-cost reducers. Proper monitoring and enforcement are essential for effectiveness.

Production, Costs, Revenues and Firm.definition: Costs that do not vary with output in the short run, such as rent or salaried administration. They must be paid even if production is zero. Average fixed cost falls as output rises due to spreading overheads.

Costs that change directly with the level of output, like raw materials and hourly wages. Total variable cost rises as more units are produced. Marginal cost is derived from changes in total variable cost.

The money a firm receives from selling its output, calculated as price times quantity (TR = P × Q). It is essential for assessing profitability and break-even. Revenue curves reflect price elasticity and market structure.

Reductions in long-run average cost as a firm expands its scale of production. Sources include technical, managerial, financial, marketing, and purchasing advantages. They allow larger firms to be more competitive.

Increases in long-run average cost when a firm becomes too large. Coordination problems, bureaucracy, and communication breakdowns can erode efficiency. It places a practical limit on optimal firm size.

Market Structures.definition: A market structure with many small firms selling identical products, free entry and exit, perfect knowledge, and no barriers to entry. Firms are price takers and produce at the allocatively efficient point where price equals marginal cost (P = MC) in the long run.

A market structure where a single firm dominates supply, often protected by high barriers to entry. The monopolist is a price maker, can restrict output to raise prices, and may produce at a point where price exceeds marginal cost (P > MC), leading to allocative inefficiency and potential deadweight loss.

A market structure with many firms selling similar but differentiated products. Firms have some price-making power due to brand loyalty or product differences. In the long run, profits are driven to normal levels due to low entry barriers, but there may still be excess capacity.

A market structure with a small number of large, interdependent firms. High barriers to entry and potential for collusion or non-price competition. Behavior is often strategic, and game theory is used to predict outcomes.

A special case of oligopoly where only two firms dominate the market. Their strategic choices strongly influence each other’s outcomes, often leading to tacit or explicit collusion, or competitive rivalry.
A monopoly that arises when a single firm can supply the entire market at a lower average cost than multiple firms due to significant economies of scale, often in utilities like water or electricity.
Markets with low entry and exit barriers, where the threat of potential competition forces incumbent firms to behave competitively, even if only one or a few firms operate at a time.

Agreements between competing firms, usually in oligopoly, to fix prices, limit production, or divide markets. They are illegal in many countries due to their anti-competitive effects and the deadweight loss they cause.

An oligopoly model suggesting that firms face a demand curve that is more elastic for price increases and less elastic for price decreases. This leads to price rigidity, as firms are reluctant to change prices for fear of losing market share or triggering price wars.
A situation in oligopoly where one firm (often the largest or most efficient) sets the price and others follow. It can be tacit collusion without explicit agreements.
An oligopoly where firms compete without formal agreements, often relying on non-price competition like advertising, branding, and product differentiation to gain market share.
An oligopoly where firms coordinate pricing and output decisions to maximize joint profits, either through explicit agreements (cartels) or tacit understandings.
Obstacles that make it difficult for new firms to enter a market, including economies of scale, brand loyalty, patents, capital requirements, and government regulation.
Barriers created by laws or regulations, such as patents, licenses, and exclusive government contracts, that limit market entry.
A measure of the market share of the largest firms in an industry, typically expressed as CR4 or CR8, indicating the percentage of total output controlled by the top 4 or 8 firms.
A game theory model where rational players, acting in their own self-interest, end up with a worse outcome than if they had cooperated. Common in oligopoly pricing decisions.
The study of strategic interactions where the outcome for each participant depends on the choices of others. It’s widely used to model behavior in oligopolistic markets.
Strategies to make a product distinct from competitors’ offerings through design, quality, branding, features, or customer service. It reduces price elasticity of demand.
Charging different prices to different customers for the same product, where the price difference is not due to cost differences. Requires market power and the ability to prevent resale.
Also called perfect price discrimination, where each customer is charged the maximum they are willing to pay, capturing all consumer surplus as profit.
Charging different prices based on the quantity purchased or product version, such as bulk discounts or premium versions.
Charging different prices to different groups of consumers based on their price elasticity of demand, such as student or senior discounts.
When firms in an oligopoly indirectly coordinate actions, such as price levels, without formal agreements, often through signaling or observing competitors’ behavior.
A tendency for prices in oligopoly to remain stable despite changes in costs or demand, often explained by the kinked demand curve model.
A pricing strategy where an incumbent firm sets a price low enough to deter entry by potential competitors, even if it sacrifices short-term profits.
A point where a market can no longer absorb more of a product due to full penetration, leading to slower sales growth and greater competition.
The strategic awareness that each firm’s decisions affect competitors’ outcomes, leading to reactive behavior and potential coordination.
Firms in perfectly competitive markets that must accept the market price determined by supply and demand, as they have no market power.
A strategic situation where one participant’s gain is exactly balanced by another’s loss. Total net benefit across players is zero.
A game theory concept where no player can improve their payoff by unilaterally changing strategy, given the strategies of others.
The horizontal summation of all individual firms’ supply curves in a perfectly competitive market. In the long run, supply adjusts to changes in demand through firm entry and exit.

Market Structures.term: Effect of Technological Change in Perfect Competition
Market Structures.definition: Technological advancements shift the cost curves downward, enabling firms to produce more at lower prices, benefiting consumers and increasing allocative efficiency.

Market Structures.term: Long Run Condition in Perfect Competition
Market Structures.definition: In the long run, firms produce where P = MC = minimum AC, earning only normal profit due to free entry and exit.
Actions by firms or individuals to increase their share of existing wealth without creating new wealth, such as lobbying for favorable regulation or exclusive rights.
When the value of a product increases as more people use it, creating a self-reinforcing demand that can support monopoly power (e.g., social media platforms).
The loss of total surplus that occurs when market output is not at the socially optimal level, often due to monopoly pricing or taxation.
Obstacles that incumbents intentionally establish to prevent competition, such as predatory pricing, exclusive contracts, or patent hoarding.
Entry obstacles arising naturally from industry characteristics, such as high fixed costs, economies of scale, or network effects.
In monopolistic competition, the idea that firms operate below the output level that minimizes average costs due to downward-sloping demand curves.
A situation where countries impose tariffs or other trade barriers on each other in retaliation, often harming both economies.
A game where all participants can be better or worse off simultaneously, allowing for the possibility of mutually beneficial cooperation.
A strategy that yields a better payoff for a player regardless of what strategies the other players choose.
A strategy that is always worse than another available strategy, regardless of opponents’ actions.

Labour Market and Income Inequalities.definition: The additional revenue a firm earns from employing one more unit of a factor of production, calculated as the marginal physical product (MPP) multiplied by the marginal revenue (MR). In a competitive market, firms hire labour up to the point where MRP equals the wage rate.

A labour market where there are many employers and workers, all with perfect information, no barriers to entry or exit, and wages determined purely by supply and demand. Individual firms are wage takers.

A market with a single buyer of labour, giving the employer wage-setting power. This can lead to lower wages and reduced employment compared to competitive markets unless regulated or countered by trade unions.

The demand for a factor of production, such as labour, that arises from the demand for the goods and services it helps produce. For example, demand for construction workers depends on demand for new housing.
The minimum wage rate at which a worker is willing to accept a job. It reflects the opportunity cost of leisure and alternative income sources.

A graphical representation showing that at low wages, higher wages encourage more labour supply, but beyond a certain point, further wage increases lead to less labour supplied as workers value leisure more.
When wages rise, the opportunity cost of leisure increases, leading workers to substitute work for leisure, thereby increasing labour supply.
When wages rise, workers earn more per hour, allowing them to afford more leisure while maintaining income, potentially reducing hours worked.
The purchasing power of wages, adjusted for inflation. It reflects the actual quantity of goods and services that can be bought with earnings.
The wage paid to workers in current money terms, without adjusting for inflation.
The wage rate at which the quantity of labour supplied equals the quantity of labour demanded, resulting in no excess supply or shortage.
The differences in wages between individuals, occupations, industries, or regions, caused by factors such as skill level, working conditions, demand, and union power.

A measure of how responsive the quantity of labour demanded is to a change in the wage rate. Influenced by the substitutability of labour, elasticity of product demand, and share of labour costs in total costs.
A measure of how responsive the quantity of labour supplied is to a change in the wage rate, affected by skill requirements, training time, and worker mobility.
The ability of workers to move between different types of jobs or industries, depending on transferable skills and qualifications.
The ability of workers to move location for employment opportunities, influenced by housing availability, transport, family ties, and cost of living.
Organizations of workers formed to protect and promote members’ interests, including higher wages, better working conditions, and job security.
A labour market situation where a single employer (monopsony) negotiates with a single trade union, resulting in wage determination through bargaining.
Legislation that sets a legal minimum wage rate, intended to protect workers from exploitation but potentially leading to unemployment if set above equilibrium wage.
An arrangement where only workers who are union members can be employed by a firm or industry.
The negotiation process between employers and trade unions over wages, working conditions, and other employment terms.
An arrangement where an employer recognizes only one union for collective bargaining purposes, simplifying negotiations.
Measures taken by workers to enforce demands, including strikes, work-to-rule, and overtime bans.
The proportion of the workforce that is unionized, indicating the strength of union representation.
A workplace where union membership is optional, and non-members can be employed.
A dispute resolution process where an independent third party helps employers and employees reach an agreement without imposing a decision.
A dispute resolution process where an independent third party listens to both sides and makes a binding decision.
Key influences include productivity, wage rate, demand for the firm’s output, price of substitutes, and technological changes.
Key influences include wage levels, working conditions, training opportunities, population size, and cultural factors.

Labour Market and Income Inequalities.term: Determinants of Elasticity of Labour Supply
Labour Market and Income Inequalities.definition: Includes skill specificity, training requirements, length of qualification time, and mobility of labour.

Labour Market and Income Inequalities.term: Determinants of Elasticity of Labour Demand
Labour Market and Income Inequalities.definition: Includes substitutability of labour, proportion of labour cost in total cost, and elasticity of demand for the final product.

National Income Accounting.definition: The total market value of all final goods and services produced within a country’s borders over a period, usually a year. It is a key gauge of economic performance and living standards. GDP can be measured by output, expenditure, or income methods.

GDP plus net income from abroad (income earned by residents overseas minus income earned domestically by non-residents). It reflects the income of a nation’s residents regardless of where production occurs. Useful for countries with large remittances or foreign investments.

GNP adjusted for depreciation (consumption of fixed capital). It indicates the sustainable income that could be consumed without reducing the capital stock. Often used for longer-term welfare assessment.
GDP adjusted for inflation using a price index to reflect changes in actual output. It enables comparisons over time by removing price effects. Growth in real GDP signals rising real output.
GDP measured at current market prices without adjusting for inflation. It may overstate growth when prices rise. Useful for understanding current market values and tax bases.

Circular Flow of Income and National Inc.definition: A model showing how money, goods, services, and factors move between households and firms. It highlights how income earned by selling factors becomes expenditure on goods and services. Injections and withdrawals shift national income.

Additions to aggregate expenditure from investment, government spending, and exports. Injections raise national income through the multiplier process. An increase in injections (ceteris paribus) expands equilibrium GDP.
Leakages from the circular flow via savings, taxes, and imports. Withdrawals reduce aggregate expenditure and national income. A rise in withdrawals (ceteris paribus) contracts equilibrium GDP.

The process by which an initial change in injections leads to a larger final change in national income. Its size depends on the marginal propensities to save, tax, and import. Higher leakages reduce the multiplier.

The total planned spending on domestic output at different price levels: C + I + G + (X − M). Components shift with confidence, interest rates, fiscal policy, and external demand. Movements in AD drive short-run changes in output and employment.

Government Macroeconomic Objectives and.definition: A sustained increase in real GDP over time. It raises incomes and can improve living standards but may bring environmental costs. Policies to promote growth include investment, education, innovation, and stable institutions.

A situation where virtually all who are willing and able to work at prevailing wages can find jobs, with only frictional and structural unemployment remaining. Achieving it may conflict with low inflation. Active labour market policies can help.
Maintaining low and predictable inflation so money retains its purchasing power. It fosters planning, saving, and investment. Central banks often target inflation to anchor expectations.

Avoiding persistent current account deficits or surpluses that could be unsustainable. Exchange rates, competitiveness, and domestic demand all influence external balance. Policies to adjust include expenditure-switching and expenditure-reducing tools.

When pursuing one macroeconomic goal makes another harder to achieve, such as reducing inflation versus unemployment. Trade-offs require prioritization and careful policy mix. Short-run versus long-run effects can differ.

Fiscal Policy.definition: Government decisions on spending and taxation used to influence economic activity. Expansionary policy boosts demand and employment; contractionary policy restrains inflation or deficits. Its effectiveness depends on multipliers and timing lags.
When government expenditure exceeds revenue in a period. It is financed by borrowing and can support demand in downturns. Persistent deficits raise public debt and interest costs.
When government revenue exceeds expenditure. It can be used to repay debt or build fiscal buffers. Large surpluses may slow growth if demand is weak.
A tax that takes a higher percentage of income from high earners. It redistributes income and can reduce inequality. However, high marginal rates may affect incentives.
A tax that takes a higher percentage from low incomes than from high incomes. Indirect taxes often have regressive effects. Governments may offset this with targeted transfers.

Monetary Policy.definition: Central bank actions—primarily setting interest rates and managing liquidity—to influence inflation, output, and employment. Lower rates stimulate borrowing and spending; higher rates cool demand. Transmission depends on banks, expectations, and global conditions.

The price of borrowing money and the reward for saving. Changes in policy rates influence consumer credit, mortgages, business investment, and exchange rates. Real (inflation-adjusted) rates matter for decisions.

A framework where the central bank announces a numerical inflation goal and uses tools to achieve it. Clear targets anchor expectations and improve credibility. Communication is crucial for effectiveness.

An unconventional tool where the central bank buys financial assets to inject liquidity and lower long-term yields. It is used when policy rates are near zero. Risks include asset bubbles and complex exit strategies.
The total stock of money in the economy, including cash and certain bank deposits. Its growth influences inflation and interest rates. Different measures (e.g., M1, M2) capture varying liquidity.

Supply-Side Policy.definition: Measures aimed at raising the economy’s productive capacity and long-run growth. They improve incentives, efficiency, and innovation. Examples include tax reform, education, infrastructure, and deregulation.

Policies to increase flexibility and participation, such as training, benefit reform, and mobility support. They can reduce structural unemployment. Effects emerge gradually and depend on implementation.
Transferring state-owned enterprises to private ownership to promote efficiency and investment. Success depends on competition and regulation. It may raise short-term revenue for government.
Reducing or simplifying rules to lower compliance costs and spur entry and innovation. Care is needed to maintain standards for safety and the environment. Often paired with competition policy.

Public spending on transport, energy, digital networks, and utilities that enable private activity. It lowers business costs and widens market access. Returns are highest when projects are well-selected and maintained.

International Economic Issues.definition: The price of one currency in terms of another, determined in foreign exchange markets. Appreciation makes imports cheaper and exports dearer; depreciation has the opposite effect. Exchange rate regimes range from fixed to floating.

When the value of imports exceeds the value of exports over a period. It can reflect strong domestic demand or competitiveness issues. Persistent deficits must be financed by borrowing or selling assets.
When the value of exports exceeds the value of imports. It may signal high competitiveness or weak domestic demand. Large surpluses can create trade tensions with partners.

Policies such as tariffs, quotas, and subsidies that restrict imports to support domestic industries. They can safeguard jobs but risk higher prices and retaliation. Long-term competitiveness may suffer without reform.

The growing integration of world economies through trade, finance, technology, and migration. It expands markets and can raise productivity, but also creates adjustment pressures. Policy support helps share the gains broadly.

Development Economics.definition: A broad improvement in living standards including higher incomes, better health and education, and reduced poverty. It goes beyond growth to consider distribution and sustainability. Institutions and governance are critical enablers.

A composite indicator combining life expectancy, education outcomes, and income per capita. It allows cross-country comparisons of development beyond GDP alone. Movements reflect both economic and social progress.

Meeting present needs without compromising future generations’ ability to meet theirs. It balances economic growth with environmental protection and social inclusion. Policy tools include regulation, green tech, and pricing externalities.

Financial and technical assistance provided by governments and institutions to support development goals. Its impact depends on governance, targeting, and complementary reforms. Aid can be grants, concessional loans, or in-kind support.

Small-scale financial services—loans, savings, insurance—offered to low-income entrepreneurs lacking collateral. It can enable business creation and resilience. Safeguards are needed to avoid over-indebtedness.
